title: Yamen and the End of Song
bullets: On March 19, 1279, the Song minister Lu Xiufu bound the eight-year-old Emperor Bing to his back with a sash and jumped into the South China Sea at the Battle of Yamen — and more than 100,000 loyalists followed him into the water.
Kublai Khan formally proclaimed the Yuan dynasty in 1271, taking the name from the Yi Jing where 'yuan' means 'origin,' and immediately claimed the Mandate of Heaven against the Southern Song.

The decisive campaign was the six-year Siege of Xiangyang, broken in 1273 only after two Persian engineers — Ismail and Ala al-Din from the Ilkhanate — built counterweight trebuchets that hurled 220-pound stones over the walls.
The Mongol-Chinese general Bayan accepted the surrender of the Song capital Hangzhou in 1276; the dowager empress handed over the imperial seals along with the six-year-old Emperor Gong.

For the first time in Chinese history, all of China was under foreign rule — the wealthiest, most populous, most technologically sophisticated state on Earth was now governed from the steppe-China frontier.
Lu Xiufu, Wen Tianxiang, and the admiral Zhang Shijie became the canonical Three Loyal Subjects of the Song, the foundational martyrdom episode of every later Chinese dynasty's loyalty ideology.

The Mongol war against north China (the Jin) had killed perhaps 40 million people; the war against the Southern Song killed a tenth of that, because Mongol policy by 1279 was to spare cities that surrendered and tax the survivors instead.

Cross-civ moment: Yamen was contemporary with the fall of Acre, the end of the Abbasid Caliphate at Mongol hands, the early Renaissance in Italy, and the founding of the Mali Empire under Sundiata Keita.
subtitle: Kublai finishes the Mongol conquest with a child emperor drowning in the sea

title: Kublai's New Capital
bullets: Kublai built Khanbaliq — Mongolian for 'city of the Khan,' Chinese Dadu, modern Beijing — as a new capital from scratch starting in 1267, far north of any previous Chinese imperial center, designed to bridge steppe and China.
The Grand Canal was extended northward to Khanbaliq between 1283 and 1293 with about 200,000 conscripted laborers, finally allowing southern rice to reach the new capital by inland water transport.

The four-class ethnic system imposed in 1271 placed Mongols on top, then Semu (Central Asian and Persian foreigners), then northern Han, then southern Han at the bottom — governing taxation, judicial penalties, and official appointments by legal caste.
Kublai used Persian and Central Asian Semu administrators like Ahmad Fanakati to run Yuan finance, deliberately inserting foreign tax-farmers between the Mongol military elite and the Han taxpayers.
The civil-service examinations were suspended in 1271 and not restored until 1315; the Han literati class spent most of the dynasty locked out of the formal scholar-official career path.

Kublai's court hosted Tibetan Buddhist monks, Nestorian Christian clerks, Sunni Muslim engineers, Confucian scholars, Persian astronomers, Italian merchants, and Catholic missionaries — the most cosmopolitan capital in pre-modern Asia.

The Beihai White Pagoda, designed by the Nepali architect Aniko in 1271, was a Tibetan-style stupa at imperial scale in the heart of the Chinese capital — the visual epitome of the Yuan synthesis, still standing.
Kublai died in 1294 at about seventy-nine, after thirty years on the throne; the Beijing capital he built has been the political center of China for the seven centuries since.
subtitle: Khanbaliq, the Grand Canal extension, and the four-class ethnic system

title: The Failed Japan Invasions
bullets: The 1274 Bun'ei invasion sailed from Korea with about 900 ships and 30,000 troops; after a day of fierce fighting at Hakata Bay the force re-embarked overnight and was caught by an autumn storm that wrecked perhaps a third of the fleet.

The 1281 Kōan invasion was the largest amphibious operation in pre-modern history — roughly 4,400 ships and 140,000 men sailing in two fleets from Korea and southern China — and it was wrecked by a major typhoon in August 1281.

Tens of thousands of Mongol and Chinese soldiers drowned or were stranded ashore at Hakata in 1281 and systematically hunted down by samurai over the following days; total losses ran to perhaps 70,000 to 100,000 dead.

The Japanese named the typhoons kamikaze, 'divine wind,' and built a national mythology around the Land of the Gods being divinely protected — a conviction that lasted six and a half centuries before being revived for the 1944–45 suicide pilots.

Modern underwater archaeology at Takashima Bay confirms that many wrecked Yuan ships were structurally substandard — hastily built by Korean Goryeo shipyards under Mongol time pressure, with inadequate keels and weak hulls.

The 1281 invasion was the high-water mark of Yuan military expansion; subsequent campaigns against Vietnam, Burma, and Java all failed, and the Mongol cavalry empire pivoted from conquest to commerce.
Goryeo Korea, a Yuan vassal since 1270, supplied most of the ships and crews and was reduced to economic ruin by the construction effort.

Cross-civ comparison: like the Spanish Armada three centuries later, both invasions show the same pattern — over-large fleet, fortified island defenders, decisive storm, and a national-religious interpretation of the storm as divine protection.
subtitle: Bun'ei, Kōan, and the typhoons that became Japan's founding myth

title: Marco Polo and the Pax Mongolica
bullets: Marco Polo arrived at Kublai's summer palace at Shangdu — Coleridge's Xanadu — in 1275 at age twenty-one, after a three-and-a-half-year overland journey of approximately 5,500 miles from Acre.

Polo served Kublai in some Yuan administrative capacity for seventeen years; his Description of the World, dictated in a Genoese prison around 1298 to romance writer Rustichello of Pisa, became one of the most influential travel narratives ever written.

Christopher Columbus carried a heavily annotated Latin copy of Polo on his voyages two centuries later — the marginal notes in Columbus's own handwriting survive in a Seville library, and Cathay was what Columbus thought he was looking for in 1492.

Rabban Sauma, a Nestorian Christian monk born in Khanbaliq, traveled west in 1287 on diplomatic mission for the Ilkhan Arghun, met Pope Nicholas IV in Rome, Philip IV in Paris, and Edward I in Bordeaux — the first known person born in China to visit western Europe.

John of Montecorvino, the Franciscan friar dispatched by Pope Nicholas IV in 1289, arrived at Khanbaliq in 1294 and stayed thirty-four years as the first Catholic Archbishop of Beijing, building two churches and translating the New Testament into a Mongol script.
Italian merchant communities at Tabriz and Khanbaliq numbered in the hundreds; Genoese and Venetian trading firms had permanent representatives at Mongol courts across Asia for roughly six decades.

The Pax Mongolica of roughly 1280–1340 was the closest thing the pre-modern world produced to a first phase of globalization — Yuan paper money circulated to Tabriz, Catholic friars trained in Italian Latin became Archbishops of Beijing.

Frances Wood's 1995 skeptical book pointed out that Polo never mentioned tea, the Great Wall, or footbinding; the mainstream response is that the absences are not as damning as they look and Polo did go to China.
subtitle: Polo, Sauma, and the most connected Eurasian moment of the pre-modern era

title: Drama, the Novel, and Yuan Vernacular Literature
bullets: Yuan zaju was a four-act theatrical form combining sung arias, spoken dialogue, and stylized stage movement; roughly 160 plays survive from perhaps 100 named playwrights.

Guan Hanqing was the most prolific Yuan playwright with about 60 plays attributed and 18 surviving; his tragedy The Injustice to Dou E — about a young widow falsely executed whose innocence is proved by snowfall in summer and three years of drought — has been continuously performed for seven centuries.

Wang Shifu's Romance of the Western Chamber became the most influential romantic drama of the period; Ji Junxiang's Orphan of Zhao would be translated into French and adapted by Voltaire in the eighteenth century.

The earliest stable forms of Romance of the Three Kingdoms and Water Margin — two of the four classic Chinese novels — date to late Yuan and early Ming, attributed to Luo Guanzhong and Shi Nai'an, written in vernacular baihua rather than classical Chinese.

Zhao Mengfu, a great-great-great-grandson of the Northern Song founder who chose to serve the Yuan, became the most influential calligrapher and painter of the dynasty — his calligraphy in regular and running scripts shaped Chinese visual culture for the next four centuries.

The Four Yuan Masters — Huang Gongwang, Wu Zhen, Ni Zan, and Wang Meng — founded the literati landscape painting tradition; Huang's Dwelling in the Fuchun Mountains, painted around 1347–1350, is the foundational masterpiece.

Song loyalist yimin painters like Zheng Sixiao painted orchids without earth around their roots, signifying lost homeland — establishing a Chinese tradition of art-as-political-protest that persisted into the twentieth century.

The dynasty's most discriminatory policy — the suspended civil-service examinations — produced its most enduring cultural legacy: vernacular drama, vernacular fiction, and literati painting all flourished because Han literati had nothing else to write.
subtitle: Guan Hanqing, the rise of the novel, and what dispossessed literati produced

title: Pax Mongolica's Plagues and Trade
bullets: Quanzhou, called Zayton in Arabic and European sources, was the busiest port in the world during the Yuan; Marco Polo claimed 100 ships entered for every one entering Alexandria, and modern excavations have uncovered hundreds of stone inscriptions in Arabic, Tamil, Syriac, Latin, and Persian.

The Moroccan jurist Ibn Battuta reached Quanzhou around 1345 during a 75,000-mile journey across Eurasia and Africa, independently confirming Marco Polo's improbable observations about Yuan paper money, the harbor's scale, and the cosmopolitan court at Khanbaliq.

The Yuan ran the world's first nationwide fiat paper currency — the jiaochao — declared the only legal tender for major transactions and accepted on pain of death; it worked for decades before collapsing into hyperinflation in the 1340s and 1350s.

Yuan kilns at Jingdezhen perfected blue-and-white porcelain using cobalt imported from Kashan in central Iran; the iconic Chinese-Islamic ceramic that defined later Ming porcelain was a Yuan-era export invention driven by Persian and Arab consumer demand.

The Yuan court astronomer Guo Shoujing's Shoushi calendar of 1281 calculated the tropical year at 365.2425 days — within 26 seconds of the modern value, and the same length the Gregorian calendar would adopt three hundred years later.

The Yersinia pestis bacterium that caused the European Black Death of 1346–1353 originated in Inner Asia and traveled west along the Mongol caravan network; the 1346 Mongol siege of the Genoese trading post at Kaffa was the immediate vector by which it reached the Mediterranean.

The Black Death killed 25 to 50 million Europeans — perhaps 30 to 50 percent of the population — and probably comparable numbers in the Middle East, North Africa, and parts of Central Asia; total Eurasian death toll over seven years was perhaps 75 to 200 million.

The same trade routes that carried Polo east and silk and porcelain west also carried the bacterium — the Pax Mongolica's commercial integration was the precondition for the deadliest pandemic in human history.
subtitle: Zayton, Ibn Battuta, paper money, and the Yersinia pestis trail

title: The Long Decline
bullets: Between Temür Öljeitü's death in 1307 and Toghon Temür's accession in 1333, the Yuan throne changed hands nine times, four emperors were assassinated, two regents were murdered, and two emperors died in 1323 within nine months of each other.

Kublai's harem succession system confined Yuan princes inside the palace under the control of empress dowagers, eunuchs, and keshig commanders — producing each generation of emperors less prepared for rule than the last.

The Yellow River broke catastrophically in 1344, drowning huge stretches of Shandong and Henan farmland and physically changing course; the redirect project under chief minister Toqto'a in 1351 conscripted 150,000 peasants under brutal conditions and the conscripts joined the rebellion that destroyed the dynasty.

Yuan paper currency, over-printed to fund military operations and famine relief, lost most of its purchasing power by the 1340s; the 1350 reform debased the notes catastrophically, ruined tax-paying peasants, and destroyed the state's fiscal capacity.

The hereditary Mongol military households of north China had become indebted, underequipped settled farmers by the 1330s; when Red Turban rebellions began in 1351, much of the Yuan army on paper could not take the field in fact.

The White Lotus Society was a millenarian Buddhist movement teaching that the future Buddha Maitreya would soon descend to overthrow the corrupt world; its organizers seeded most of the late-Yuan rebellions and provided the Red Turbans their ideological framework.

The Red Turban rebellions exploded in 1351 across Henan, Anhui, and Shandong; by 1356 multiple regional warlords controlled most of central and southern China, and the Yuan central state could no longer mount a coordinated counter-insurgency.

The fourteenth-century crisis was a single Eurasian phenomenon — the Black Death, peasant unrest, fiscal collapse, and political fragmentation hit the Yuan, the Ilkhanate, the Mali Empire, and Avignon Europe in the same generation.
subtitle: Yellow River disasters, hyperinflation, and the millenarian uprising that finished the dynasty

title: Zhu Yuanzhang and the Ming Founding
bullets: Zhu Yuanzhang was born in 1328 to landless peasants in Anhui, lost his parents and brother to plague and famine at sixteen, became a Buddhist novice, spent three years as a wandering mendicant monk, and joined the Red Turban army at twenty-four — the only Chinese founding emperor of pure peasant birth.

Zhu took Nanjing in 1356 and made it his capital; over the next twelve years he systematically defeated every other rival in central and southern China, including Chen Youliang at the Battle of Lake Poyang in 1363 and Zhang Shicheng at Suzhou in 1367.

The Battle of Lake Poyang involved several thousand ships and was decided when a stray arrow killed Chen Youliang during a major engagement — one of the largest naval battles in pre-modern world history.

On January 1368 Zhu Yuanzhang proclaimed the Ming dynasty at Nanjing and took the reign name Hongwu, 'Vastly Martial'; on September 14, 1368, Toghon Temür abandoned Khanbaliq overnight and fled north into Mongolia, ending the Yuan as a Chinese dynasty after 97 years.

The rump Northern Yuan continued to claim the imperial title from Mongolia for two centuries and remained a security threat; the Great Wall as we know it was largely a Ming defensive response to continuing Mongol pressure.

Hongwu's son the Yongle Emperor moved the Ming capital back to Beijing in the 1420s and built the Forbidden City on the foundations of Kublai's Khanbaliq; the Beijing layout that Chinese politics has been organized around for seven centuries is Kublai's city.

The Yuan's permanent legacies include Beijing as China's capital, Tibetan Buddhism as a major state religion, the vernacular literary tradition, the multi-ethnic territorial conception of China, and the institutional model of foreign-dynasty Chinese governance.

Modern Chinese historiography is split three ways on whether the Yuan was a foreign occupation, a legitimate Chinese dynasty, or a Mongol Eurasian empire that incidentally governed China — the question is unresolved and probably unresolvable.
subtitle: the peasant-monk founder, the 1368 expulsion, and what the Yuan left permanently behind
